entry.title: The (pretty) big biking post
entry.summary: Hey so I actually wrote all this up two weeks ago but never came around to finish it. I am currently in my midterm period, so time is in fact not the most plentiful. Even though everyone says that you will litteraly pass by just…
entry.content:
Hey so I actually wrote all this up two weeks ago but never came around to finish it. I am currently in my midterm period, so time is in fact not the most plentiful. Even though everyone says that you will litteraly pass by just showing up, I still feel stressed about it. By Wednesday, after my three tests, everything will be over atleast.

Anyways, enjoy my two-week old blogpost in the meantime - by next week, I will update you on all the fun stuff happening!

Please read this about biking i beg you i swear its interesting

As I said in a previous post, my dream here is to do a long bike trip, preferrably to Seoul. To start off a bit easy, I tried going to Gyeongju the other week, about 50 km round trip. That went really good, and I felt really confident for the longer trip, wherever it took me. There were some really good bike routes along the river, and whenever I had to bike on car roads, everyone felt really kind and didn't go too fast. The plan originally was to bike all the way to Seoul starting thursday afternoon last week. I prepared with routes and was looking up motels and everything on the way. Unfortunately, the air quality was like extremely bad and I did not really want to go all the way to Seoul in full smog. What I tried to do instead was to bike towards Busan. I took the scenic route along the ocean, which would have taken about 2 days of biking in total. Unfortunately, about 40 km in, I got a forest fire warning in my phone and there were forest fire cars driving past me. I tried to wave one down but instead of stopping the woman just smiled and waved and drove past. Apparently it was fine, but I didn't want to take any risks. I biked back to Pohang instead. It was a good first try of the road though, and I really want to try again soon. Stay tuned for that, and for the Seoul bike trip.

Other things that happened during the two weeks

  • I went to Daegu. Daegu is the 3rd or 4th largest city in the country, and everyone here kinda jokes about Daegu that there's nothing to do there or whatever. I have to admit - I was defintely one of them before going there. However, I am now changed. Daegu is a really cool city, and I loved the vibes there. Both me and my roomie Jens agreed that if we were to live here long term, Daegu would be one of the top spots to do so. They also had a huge tech mall with a bunch of camera stores, PC shops and almost everything you could imagine. Since I live for those kind of places, I felt like a kid in a (very expensive) candy store. It was great. Something they didn't have though was MP3 players and Sony Walkmans. Fortunately, Daegu blessed us again with an old man in a random market shop. I found a lil beauty for waaaaay below ebay price. Shoutout mr. old Daegu man. I hope he's doing great.
  • We went hiking close to Pohang. There's a trail called the 12 waterfalls, where you just hike along a mountain pass and see a bunch of waterfalls. It was really cool. The trees still haven't gotten that many leaves, so it definitely looked a bit naked on the trail. Bummer but I guess we just have to go back another time to see it again.
  • We went to Gyeongju world, like an amusement park half an hour away from Pohang. It was a blast. Somehow the queues were way shorter than what I'm used too. The rides were also really good, and snack prices were reasonable. Overall a very good experience, and the price with taxis included was not even entry+free rides back home.

Jens, Ludo and Karro, tell em

I saw this once in Gyckeln and I thought it was a really funny concept, so here is my blatant ripoff of the concent. We all need some guidance in the current trend landscape. To help us in this task, I interviewed two people:

  • Jens-David Olabode, maybe Denmarks biggest trendsetter and zeitgeist icon. He has a solid 21 years in the style game, and never misses a beat.
  • Ludvig Olof Lindefeldt, most peaceful and chill known to man. Rumors say he was sent to Islamabad for peace negotiations. By following this man's teachings, even you might reach your inner trend zen.
  • Karolina Hedberg, knows what's up all the way between Skellefteå and Göteborg. Among the widest reaches in all of Sweden, both geographically and figuratively.

The premise is simple. A thing or concept can be:

  • Hot: You want this. The thing is solidly in and will be so for the foreseeable future.
  • Cooling down: The thing is currently hot, but will not stay so for much longer.
  • Not: The thing is not hot. You would not want to touch it with a 10 meter stick.
  • Heating up: somewhere in between hot and not, and definitely on the uptick. You are ahead of the curve, so to say.

Anyways, here are their ratings on some things and concepts: 

  Jens-David Karolina Ludo
Pink Buldak Noodles solidly hot. No signs of going anywhere. Cooling down. Too hot(spicy)

Hot 

Jens: this man is crazily addicted to this
Daegu Heating up. People are sleeping on Daegu. They just don't know the beauty of the city. Heating up Cooling down. Everyone wanted to go in the beginning, but no one anymore. Mentioned that he was the first Daegu goer.
Seoul Haven't been to the city, so cannot tell really. Hot until proven otherwise. Hot Heating up
Busan Kinda lukewarm at the moment. Might even be cooling down a bit. Not Heating up

Korean BBQ

 as hot as the BBQ grill we are cooking on. Not a change this will change. Cooling down. Has had too much. Lukewarm. It was 2 years ago.
Vegetarianism and veganism In Korea, not. Back home, hot. Not/heating up We’re in Korea. It’s not and still it’s cooling down
Second hand clothing markets HOT. Will buy an extra suitcase. Neither price nor space is an issue. Extremely in. Hot Cooling down. We don’t do that anymore. He got CSN the other day and is now going to Lotte Mall instead.
MP3 players Very hot. Has considered buying one. Spotify is over. Cooling down. Hot
Mobile phones Not. Why would you need a phone when you have an mp3 player and a camera? Cooling down. Cooling down.
Jimjilbangs   Not, but should be heating up. Definitely heating up
Log Cabin   Always in style. Timeless Heating up
Cafeteria breakfasts Hot, but only because they’re cheap. If they were the same price as the other meals, a solid not. Heating up We don’t do that. Cooling down.

entry.title: Me getting into routine but the routine is just extremely chill and it's just fantastic
entry.summary: Whats up yall this is probably the longest streak I have ever consistently updated the blog ever! Huge celebration inbound for this one Anyways, last week has been pretty good. Classes are still somewhat boring and just full of repetition, but I hope that starting…
entry.content:
Whats up yall this is probably the longest streak I have ever consistently updated the blog ever! Huge celebration inbound for this one

Anyways, last week has been pretty good. Classes are still somewhat boring and just full of repetition, but I hope that starting from next week (maybe), there will be new things in all the classes. In the ones with new things, everything is also surprisingly easy. Like, I have barely studied outside of class easy. I talked to a guy who did an exchange in Taiwan about it and he had the same feelings - he said that that's kinda the norm in Asia. I don't know how true that is but definitely an interesting that it's like this in both Korea and Taiwan. This definitely goes against all my prejudices regarding east asians and studying. Anyways, the content is mostly very interesting. It's basically like I'm listening to podcasts about semiconductor technology instead of actually doing any work. Fantastic.

I also love that Fridays are free. Since there is basically (for now) no studying at all, I just get a three-day weekend. Which is so incredibly nice if you actually want to travel somewhere. So much less stress for weekend trips, and like - it's just possible to go  places. Since Korea is such a small country, that makes it even better.

Speaking of  weekend trips, this time we went to Seoul. It was great. We rented an apartment close to Hongdae. The area seems kinda like Södermalm in vibes. Lots of male manipulators and hipster places around. Coming from Montreal, I felt right at home. I mostly hung out in a big flea market area, looking for a Walkman MP3 player. While I had a great time looking through multiple-story shops filled from floor to ceiling of digital cameras and Nintendo DS lites and going through piles of 2000KRW (=13SEK) clothes, I didn't find a single MP3 player. I am close to giving up and just looking for it when (stay tuned!) I go to Japan, or ordering on Ebay. Because it seemed like the prices were pretty much as you can find online. Other than that, we went out clubbing, had Korean BBQ and went to a night market. And man, that night market took a lot of work to find. We had found one place on the internet, which seemed good. When we got there, there was absolutely nothing there. We asked so many strangers, including a policeman giving us a lecture on not smoking in a public area. He actually gave us some directions on where to go, but we never found anything. After maybe 30 mins of just wandering around aimlessly, we kinda gave up and took the bus back to Myeongdong, no. 1 tourist spot in Seoul. We had a great time there, being hungry from all the wandering and searching. Hotteok is a fantastic snack.

Yesterday, I also took my bike for it's first real ride. I biked for about 10km along the river, and came across this super beautiful traditional village. It's Unesco protected and everything. The plan is to bike all the way to Gyeongju and back on Friday. That should be around 30km one way. We'll see if I succed and if theres an okay bike lane all the way there or not. Stay tuned for the issue!

Anyways I hope you have a wonderful week back in Sweden/Europe in large/North America. Love yall puss puss <3

Photodumping time

Seoul Trip

We were actually thinking of stealing the aprons, but then we felt bad and asked if we could get one instead. The owner was super happy and actually gave us two new ones. Morel of the story is that you should always ask before stealing anything

Yangdong Cultural Village

I think I  came at the wrong time, because apart from a few people it was a complete ghost town. People actually still live here apparently. There's a school and everything.

Other things and thoughts and stuff

There are some weird clothing brands here that are like super popular for some reason. For example, everyone wears National Geographic and it's like very expensive to buy new. Luckily, me and Jens found one fleece each at a flea market in Busan for not that much. We are pretty much assimilated now.

entry.title: Second Days in Korea
entry.summary: Whats up team it has now been almost four weeks since I landed here and I have been settling in and everything. My courses are starting to move past repetition, I went on a trip to Busan, and I have bought a bike. Many a…
entry.content:
Whats up team it has now been almost four weeks since I landed here and I have been settling in and everything. My courses are starting to move past repetition, I went on a trip to Busan, and I have bought a bike. Many a thing happening.

Busan Trip

It was a cool city. I honestly liked Seoul a lot more, felt easier to get around in and just generally less cars etc. Unpopular opinion maybe but yeah. There was some really good second hand shopping in the markets in Busan though. We also went out clubbing the first night - really cool place. We actually got rejected for being foreigners at the first place though, so that was a totally new experience.

Day two was the aforementioned shopping and going to the spa time. I will talk more about that later.

Day three was Buddha's birthday, which we were totally unprepared for. We went to a really cool temple on the coast, along with seemingly every single buddhist in East Asia. It was a cool temple but way to many people. I guess us coming and touristing did not help the fact. The day was finished off with a trip to Busan's IKEA. I can confirm that they have meatballs. However, no lingondricka or flädersaft. You can't win every fight I guess.

Erik discovering Korean Spas

Alright so this was something recommended by Madeline before I went, but man this is just amazing. It's both a spa part and a relax part, and in total costs like 18 000 KRW (=100SEK ish). You enter, undress completely and just sit in hot baths butt naked with some old korean men. You don't understand them and they don't understand you. It's great. I was at one in Pohang first, and also at a much larger one in Busan. And honestly, I liked the Pohang one more. Busan just felt too big and was more just a bathhouse with kids running around everywhere. Nothing for real Korean intellectuals like myself.

In the relax area, theres like places to take a nap with different temperatures, massage chairs and (sometimes) actual massage places. You can also get snacks and noodles while inside. And craziest thing - you can just stay the night and sleep in the spa if you feel like it ??!! Top ten crazy things ever. When I go on my bike trip to Seoul that will definitely totally happen I could feasibly just find a spa and chill for a couple of hours and then go to sleep in the same place, for no extra cost. Absolute bonkers. Anyways I know what business I will be starting when I come back to Sweden. Who cares about engineering physics?

The very real bike trip that I will totally do // Buying a bike second-hand

A case study on Korean culture and why old Korean men scare me sometimes

One of my large goals when in Montreal was to bike across the border south, and continue down into New York. I calculated that it should take less than a week to do. Anyways, due to unforeseen circumstances, I am no longer in Montreal and cannot finish that goal. Instead my goal is now to do a similar thing in Korea - I'll cross the border to the socialist brothers in the north and hang out in Pyeongyang for a couple of days. I heard they love biking up there.

In case that goal doesn't work out either, I guess Seoul can suffice. There's a great cross-country bike road that is more or less paved the entire way. My plan is to take the bus to Daegu and bike the remaining 450 km up to Seoul in about 3.5 days. Will I make it all the way in that amount of time? Possibly, I honestly do not know. We'll see. But the first step in any bikepacker's dream is browsing reddit for advice buying a bike. My Korean friend helped me on the second hand site and everything. We found a nice looking bike, and contacted the guy. Turns out - he wasn't home at the time, but since the bike stood outside with a codelock, he offered to sell it anyways through a money transfer. My #1 king Changhee pulled through yet again and helped me with the korean bank transfer so that I could get my hands on the bike. I am pretty sure it's a carbon fiber frame, which is absolutely crazy for the 1400SEK price that I paid. 

Anyways, transaction is complete and I walk away with my new bike in hand. And here is where my very interesting cultural observations come:

  • I had to pump the tyres, so I found the nearest bike shop still open on a sunday night. The guy there doesn't speak any english, and I obviously do not speak any Korean, but I could signal to him that I needed a pump. He follows me outside and actually like starts yelling angrily at me in Korean because I cannot comprehend how his silly pump contraption works. I still do not understand if I did something wrong or not, but he just seemed pissed to have a foreigner in his shop. Just like refuse me service or something instead then? Cmon gramps
  • A lot of things in Korea are very clean in the moment, but veeeeery poorly maintained and upkept. Many facades could use a lift for example. Streets and sidewalks are another example. There were so many cracks and potholes that I felt motionsick for a part of the bike trip. Hopefully the cross-country road is better upkept atleast. But the priorities are interesting coming from a country focusing more or less on the direct opposite - very few places in Sweden look genuinely run-down, even in lower-income areas, but trash on the street and general cleanliness is way lower than here.
  • bro just move your ass youre walking four ppl in teh bike lane looking straight at me while the pedestrian lane is litteraly 2m to your right just let me bike past genuinely what is wrong with you
  • there were genuinely multiple times where I was close to running into someone because people just don't have any sense of their surroundings here. I am not the only cyclist in Pohang by any stretch, but they just couldn't for the life of me move out of the damm way.

Anyways that was all my cultural observations for this week. Stay tuned for next week's foreigner unfriendliness observations in this fantastic country.

entry.title: First Days in Korea
entry.summary: Man this place is so cool and theres just so many things to explore and everything and its so cool and i love it and yeah Theres so much to write about and stuff but basically everything is off to a really good start I…
entry.content:
Man this place is so cool and theres just so many things to explore and everything and its so cool and i love it and yeah

Theres so much to write about and stuff but basically everything is off to a really good start I think. Most of the courses seem really good and the university campus is just kinda crazy. For now it's still definitely in the honeymoon phase, so I don't know how I will feel in a month, but I love it here. There is a school cafeteria and shared rooms in a dorm and everything. Man it's crazy how Korea feels more American than Canada.

Yesterday I went to a traditional Korean spa, called a jimjilbang (? spelling). It basically one big naked party where you sit in a sauna with a bunch of completely naked Korean men being everything from 6 to 80 years old. Best thing was just talking to the old Korean men and laughing while basically none of us understand what the other is saying. Shoutout breaking language barriers by being butt ass naked. Today I had my first courses. Honestly they seem waaay better than in Montreal, though there are basically only Koreans in my classes. Me and one german guy is going strong in two courses though.

Here are some photos that I took with my camera the past two weeks. They first ones are from Seoul and the four last ones are from Pohang. Hope you enjoy <3

 

And here are the phone photos including (one) cafeteria photo (!!!)

entry.title: A collection of the last two month's happenings
entry.summary: Hello blog hope yall have missed my long awaited return for the like 3rd time! Here is a snippet of what's been happening for the last while. I have indeed met most of the ppl in the groupchat already, so most things aren't a big…
entry.content:
Hello blog hope yall have missed my long awaited return for the like 3rd time! Here is a snippet of what's been happening for the last while. I have indeed met most of the ppl in the groupchat already, so most things aren't a big surprise - but here is the rundown.

Last time in Montreal

I actually forgot what I wrote in the last post, but looking back, it summarizes the feeling quite well. Looking back, the entire semester just felt very temporary and I had a hard time actually creating a connection there. I really liked the vibe of the city and I met some wonderful people there. I just wish it didn't have such a rough start, and that I could actually meet and hang out with people in the beginning instead of the very end. Up until the last exam, the days looked quite same-y. Wake up -> study -> eat something -> sleep. I had a group project with two fr*nch ppl who did litteraly nothing until the last day where they tried to help with ChatGPT. That was fun to do like two days before one of the exams. Anyways, exam weeks passed and I passed the courses. I don't ever have to see those guys again. All is well and good.

After school was done, I had other things to think about. First and foremost, we had our last hangouts and goodbye dinner in the apartment. I got to try Raclette for the first time like 3 times during the last ten days. Absolutely delish. Eventually, one by one, we started to leave Montreal for christmas. It was a rather weird feeling in the end, being all alone in the apartment that I

yeah i cant bother to write more here can't even lie. Some honorable mentions from montreal are:

  • bikes
  • premier mousson for letting me study with célian every day without paying like ever
  • bernie beignes for making the best donuts ive ever had
  • ZEEZ burgers for making burgers so good and fatty i got tummyaches everytime i ate there
  • the national archive library thing that i studied in approximately once
  • MIT opencourseware for making me pass quantum mechanics
  • Nicolas Quesada for being one of the chillest professors I've ever had

anyways heres the photodump

asså jävla bajsprogram fan va jag hatar det här kan du bara funka för fan SNÄLLA

okay so theres a lot more pictures but i cant even insert them bc this program is stupid core

ohyeah i also went to california that was really cool

anyways a lot more has happened but im too mad at this program to continue right now its currently midnight in Korea. Anyways stay tuned for the next update of Publii where I can hopefully add pictures like normal again!! Puss och kram <3

entry.title: The weekend of 48 is in fact over
entry.summary: Hey yall! 4 week late Erik here with another blog update! As expected, november was quite gloomy and boring. More or less nonstop studying, like 10 hours per day for weeks on end. Last weekend was the first since Madeline came to visit seven weeks…
entry.content:
Hey yall! 4 week late Erik here with another blog update!

As expected, november was quite gloomy and boring. More or less nonstop studying, like 10 hours per day for weeks on end. Last weekend was the first since Madeline came to visit seven weeks ago that I didn't spend a day studying. So, the experience hasn't been the best since then. I really miss the mode of education at Uppsala. Here, we just do loads of classes with no practice sessions at all. So you "learn" a bunch of subject with no depth at all and no knowledge of how to use them. Either that, or there are endless labs where you troubleshoot the professor's awfully written code. Some snow came a couple of weeks ago and actually stayed for a good week and a half. That was nice. Then it got warmer and back to endless rainy and cold november days. My dad also came to visit two weeks ago, bringing loads of swedish food and candy. That was also really nice. At that point I was glad that I had studied so hard the weeks prior that I actually had some time to spend with him.

Another thing that definitely brightened my mood a bit was the exchange fair that the school organized. Essentially I had a stand where I yapped for a good 3 hours about how Uppsala is the best town every and how everyone should go there. I also met a really cool guy there that went on an exchange to Uppsala last spring. Too bad we have no time to hang out because of the stupid amounts of studies that we have to do. That has been a running theme the past month. There has been things I've wanted to do, like just exploring the city more, going on smaller concerts, musicals, whatever, but I have just not had neither the time nor the energy to actually to that and look up anything. My days have just consisted of going to school for the class of the day, and then going back to the apartment to study more/cook etc. I'm extremely thankful for having such nice flatmates that I can hang out with as much as possible atleast. But it has definitely been very tiring and isolating. We were gonna go on a trip to a lodge in the forest a week ago, but I ended up missing that due to a small cold that turned super awful presumably due to burnout or something. I was OUT that weekend.

At this point I just want the exchange to be over. I worked so hard to get here, just for it to end up awful because turns out french people are quite unfriendly towards non-french people(shocker!), and that my visa got hella delayed to the point that I missed all the welcome activities. And turns out, Korean visa application is like super duper easy and takes only two weeks or something. I can even apply here in Montreal. Quebec, please take notes when you gain independence.

I'm so ready to go home and not interact with a single french person for a good year or so.

Eriks concluding remarks and and observations

  • This town is so incredibly hipster. It's basically like one big Södermalm. There is a new cafe that serves 6$ coffee in like every corner. Also, like every place in my vicinity has merch ?? Surely not that many people want to buy merch from the local bagel shop/donut shop/pizzeria/café ? Some of it does look kinda cool, but I'd never wear a tshirt saying Saint-Viateur bagel shop on it in Montreal. So maybe they bank on short-term residents buying t-shirts to seem hip and different when they leave town. I will sure be one of them. 
  • I got my hands on a super cool Quebec jersey !!! Litteraly best purchase like every ong fr !! I also got a bunch of stickers for Quebec independence at the same time !! Write in chat if want when I go back home !! It all came from the organization Projet Équipe Nationale, that are basically undraising for a national hockey team for Quebec. At some points it's kinda crazy how alive the independence movement is here. When I think of which country I am in, the first thing coming to mind is definitely Quebec rather than Canada. It's just prevalent everywhere. It's also kinda crazy how hardcore some people are about independence. There is a guy that I share two classes with that only talks French in class. And it's not like he cannot speak english -- I have spoken english with him without any problems. The classes are also in english. But whenever he asks a question, it's always in French. Sometimes he also comes to class with a Quebec flag tshirt. Hope bros having an amazing day.
  • this take is redacted due to the risk of offending french people
  • Building upon the hipster thing, keffiyehs are like a normal thing to wear as a piece of clothing here. I don't really know what to think about that. For one, of course it's good to show support for the cause. But at the same time, it kinda defeats the purpose if it just turns into a fashion symbol rather than an actual symbol of support and tie back to the roots of resistance. Though, if there is anywhere where it is actually justified it would be here, just based on the sheer number and sizes of palestine protests in this city. Also the white boy with a saviour complex should probably stop talking about cultural appropriation now.

entry.title: The weekend of week 44 is almost over!
entry.summary: Hello blog! Another week, another blogpost!! This week has for sure had its ups and downs. All my midterms are done, and I have basically no school work (for now) except for catching up on everything I've missed from before. However, this doesn't stop me…
entry.content:
Hello blog! Another week, another blogpost!!

This week has for sure had its ups and downs. All my midterms are done, and I have basically no school work (for now) except for catching up on everything I've missed from before. However, this doesn't stop me from having lots to stress and worry about -- in November I will have fifteen (15) assignments. Crazy to think that all these professors cannot coordinate and spread out the work for the courses that they know exchange students pick together, even though being litteral colleagues and and sharing the same offices.

I'm also definitely feeling the fact that it's more and more autumn-y now. I've had the months of the nice mild early Montreal autumn now, with pretty warm weather and a long period of trees turning yellow/red and everything generally feeling quite vibey. But as with every late autumn comes the late autumn depression. The leaves are starting to turn brown and are wiltering away. It's getting colder, both outside and in the apartment (as the landlord still hasn't turned on the heating). It's more rainy and darker outside. All the fun things I've planned so far is kinda done and over, and what's left until christmas is just kinda to do school work. November kinda always feels like this, just dark and boring and something you have to wait out until december comes around and makes people automatically more happy. It doesn't get better by the fact that I have crazy much schoolwork and basically won't really have any free time. I really want to be one of those that enjoy the autumn, which I do to an extent. But, when november rolls around it's always just dark and bleak and cold and wet and boring. It feels a lot like the Bob Hund song helgen v.48. The time is just kinda passing by and I'm just waiting for the weekend of week 48 to be over so that December can roll around and make everyone happy again.

Maybe I should've gone to the southern hemisphere for my exchange instead, and just chill out during argentinian spring or something. 

I'm also very much missing Sweden and my life back home at this point. I miss living in a city that okay-ish sized where there's things to do all the time and that feels more spontaneous. I miss my friend network back home. I miss studying at Carolina Rediviva and other old campuses. I being able to go to school or Kalmar and just know that there'll be some friends I can hang out there. Shoutout to nations man they definitely help the student town vibe dramatically. Of course I knew about how the student life was ish in Stockholm before, but I never really realized how much they help the social life in the city. Not only the really cheap bars and clubs, but also the kind of "third space" where you can just hang out with and meet people. Even though Montreal sure is a big city with a lot of things happening, it's not that student focused from what I see, and I definitely don't have the friend network from back home that I could just do dumb stuff with at any point that I wanted. Anyways, when I come back I demand that there shall be a big party. Any hosting abilities in Uppsala is very appreciated. 

There were mainly two things except for  school that happened this week:

La Ronde Amusement Park

Last Sunday me and my friend Lauren went to the local amusement park, for the last day of their halloween special opening thingymajig. It was alright, but honestly a bit too little scary people walking around. Apparently they were way more intimidating earlier years, with (chainless) chainsaws and everything, but it felt quite tame this time around. Maybe it was just the last day and they didn't really have anything left, or cost cutting measures, or the evil politically correct mafia has struck again and stolen their chainsaws. Hard to tell which. Anyways, the rides were pretty cool. It was an amusement park alright. I do think Liseberg in Göteborg is alot better though -- vibe is better and  cooler rides. I miss Balder and Helix </3

At the midpoint we also had a Beaver Tail, which is a (traditionally Canadian ? don't know) pastry consisting of fried dough and some kind of topping. Think like a churros kinda thing, but it's flat and looks like a beaver tail. I had apple pie filling topping on mine. It was actually a lot better than I expected, even though I don't like churros that much. I'd give it a solid 7/10.

Parc Omega, and short roadtrip

Last Tuesday, I went with two other from the apartment and some friends of friends to a huge wilderness zoo kinda thing, called Parc Omega. The day started off really early, when we had to get to the airport to pick up the rental car. By the time we get there it was a huge hassle just to get to the car rental companies including walking across the entire airport and taking the shuttle and everything. About an hour after arrival to the airport, we finally find the rental place, just to find out that the booking we made was invalid and cancelled. Luckily there was another car that we could rent that fit everyone, and we were off.

After about two hours of driving, including lots of Veronica Maggio by demand of Swedish music, we cross the border very quickly into Ontario and stop at a border town to buy groceries. First thing we notice is the Canadian politeness. It was honestly quite crazy how big of a difference there was. The cashier lady just went on and on with small talk, and was so interested in us going to Parc Omega. I hope she has a great day. After buying things for the baguettes that we had bought earlier at the local french bakery in Montreal (because obviously Canadian baguettes would not suffice for French ppl) and some chips, we went on the last stretch, back into Quebec, arriving at the park just a short bit after.

The actual consisted of a trail that you drive on, with loads of wild animals along the way. I'm talking deers, moose, boars, bears, wolves and foxes. First thing we see when we get into the park was a huge collection of deers just waiting for the cars that drive in to feed them with carrots. Animal ethics aside, it was a really cool experience. Apparently they do actually take care of the animals in there and let them live semi-naturally, but if your name is Sandro, please look away. Essentially, they are held in captivity but have all the animals neccesary to kind of simulate an ecosystem kinda thingymabob.

Recipe, as promised from last week - Eriks kinda original tofu shawarma

I was really craving shawarma a couple of weeks ago. However, there aren't that many shawarma/kebab places in town. At least no places with the swedish tio minuter en kvart vibe -- they're all kinda too bougie, and I do not trust a bougie shawarma place run by hipster white people. I'm also trying to buy as little meat as possible, and wanted to try new ideas with tofu. So, here's instead a recipe by a hipster white guy doing very untraditional tofu shawarma!

You will need:

  • 400 g extra firm tofu, preferrably genuine Quebecois
  • 2 tbsp ml soy sauce
  • 2 tbsp oyster sauce 
  • 2 tbsp oil (I use canola oil)
    Realistically, this is just your standard tofu marinating ingredients. Change as you like.
  • 1-2 dl yoghurt/sourcream
  • Juice from 1/2 lemon
  • Shawarma spice mix
  • (if preferred) Other spices, such as cayenne pepper
  • Some kind of flour (e.g wheat, maize, potato)

Steps to make it

  1. Grate the tofu with a box grater on the large side into a big bowl.
  2. Add all the other ingredients except the flour, making sure to add a generous amount of shawarma spice mix. Blend and let sit to marinate for at least 30 minutes.
  3. Add a bit of flour and blend again. Spread out thinly on a baking tray covered in a baking sheet. Preferrably, only one layer thick of tofu.
  4. Put in the oven on the broil mode (ie. heat only from the top element) as high as it can go. Cook until the edges are starting to burn.
  5. Done! Serve as you wish, eg. liba bread with vegetables and sauce, in a taco bread with fries if youre fr*nch 🤢, or as a taco sandwich.

Make vegan: Change yoghurt for vegan yoghurt, and oyster sauce for vegan oyster sauce. Make gluten-free: do not use wheat flour.

Other random things on my mind 

"Digital" versus actually owning things

Before I left for Canada, I just had a craving to have a physical cookbook. Of course, I didn't really have to crave, as there was a copy of Bonniers Stora Kokbok from the 1980s ish sitting in my bookshelf, just screaming to be used, while I just use Arla or ICA websites whenever I need recipes. So that was for sure kind of on me.

However, it goes kinda further than that. I really want to try to own more things in my life. Be it, having mp3 files intead of spotify, setting up my own cloud service where I have full control of the actual files instead of Google Drive, Google Photos or Onedrive etc

My biggest dream is to have my own server in the closet of a future apartment, where I can just kinda have everything I want. It's not really about preserving data or whatever, but I just like the thought of actually having the files instead of using a streaming service or something similar. Realistically, what difference does it make? Not that much. But it just feels better. Also, it prevents big tech companies from spying on your photos and training AI models of your listening and watching habits, and creating even better AI slop machines from the photos and videos on your phone. Scawy stuff.

It also ties into the whole attention economy debacle and choosing yourself what to watch or listen to. It feels more human that way.  I've been trying to cut down on social media use as much as possible this year. And while it works out pretty well in Sweden, it's definitely a lot harder when I'm abroad. I've had to redownload instagram just because some student organizations have group chats there. It's quite frustrating really -- you can do as much as you want yourself, but in the end you're kinda tied to whatever the people around you are using if you want to stay in touch. Maybe I should just leave society and return to monkey. I've also wanted to use a dumbphone for a while now, but that's even more out of reach. For now, Whatsapp is kind of a requirement which complicates things alot. In Sweden, Swish and stuff I could realistically live without, by just having physical cash on me, or sending the swish when I get home from a smartphone that I just have swish/bankid. However, here I'm kinda stuck with using Whatsapp for more or less all communication. There are some feature phones that advertise whatsapp compatibility, but as far as I've seen, support is dropped as of recently. When I come back to Sweden in June, I will definitely try harder, but for now a smartphone is very important to have.

Yeah actually i just had one thing on my mind and that was it

Anyways, until next sunday, hope yall have a good november week that is not too depressing! Mwah! 

me and the coloc trying to find halloween outfits

entry.title: The big list and ranking post
entry.summary: Hello blog! Hope yall are doing great<3 I have had a couple of very hectic weeks behind me. First, a full week of only studying and a midterm before the break, then Madline came, and then a break week, consisting of studying for 10h/day more…
entry.content:
Hello blog! Hope yall are doing great<3

I have had a couple of very hectic weeks behind me. First, a full week of only studying and a midterm before the break, then Madline came, and then a break week, consisting of studying for 10h/day more or less to catch up. Meanwhile, my neighbours have been going on week long camping trips and visiting Boston and everything. Mechanical  and computer engineers amirite 

This week has been a bit more chill, but I also had two midterms on the same day, and a couple of projects that needed to be handed in. Polytechnique better be happy that i enjoy physics from this amount of work >:( Anyways, now all the midterm exams and reports are done, and I can finally rest for the weekend! So, in no particular order, here are events and things that have happened since the last post:

  • I saw two concerts: Oklou and Yung Lean. Very cool. About the same audience for both, but way more well-behaved at the Oklou concert. See below for more deets!!!!
  • I did two midterm exams, of which one was at 03:00. I just love kvantmekanik 10hp avancerad nivå so much <3
  • I have started watching the K-drama Hometown Cha Cha Cha with Madeline. It is incredibly cheesy and we mostly just laugh over how stupid it is. But also very fun. Recommend!
  • Also yeah im going back to Sweden after new year's and going to Pohang in South Korea to study next semester. Don't have that much more info as of now but yeah it is happening!
  • I went around street photographing today in a park and there were SO MANY SQUIRRELS like they were litteraly everywhere and just chilling with humans. On one park bench, a woman was litteraly invaded by squirrels climbing all over her. They are also very photogenic, as can be studied in Figure [1].

Okay here is the list with all the lists

wow its almost like a tensor if you think about it

Concerts

  • Viagra Boys -- Crazy good show. Everyone was just moshing and having a grand old time. I got to sing in their microphone and it was overall just a surreal experience. Also plus points for me not being ready at all to go and just buying the tickets kind of on a whim the same night. The setlist was incredibly good, with a mix between basically all the albums, and it was great chilling with everyones' uncles and aunts. The opening band BOODEGA was also really good, though when they told us they were from New York, everyone just kinda looked at each other a bit awkwardly. Quebecois people are so funny. Verdict: 10/10.
  • Oklou -- Speaking of buying tickets on a whim, I bought tickets to this concert litteraly like 10mins before it started. I had totally forgotten and was ready to study for like and hour and go to sleep early. But instead, I got the calendar notification that it was her show, and hopped onto ticketmaster to find a resale ticket. Luckily, there were tickets for not that much, and I zoomed 7km to the other side of town to make it just in time to catch the last part of the dj opener, which for once was actually really good. She was also amazing. Emotional, lots of silly in-between talk, great performing and very cool stageplay. Amazing crowd, seemingly consisting of drainers and lesbian montrealers. When she asked which language she should speak on stage, everyone shouted FRANCAIS. Loved it. 9/10 just because I couldn't see more of the opener.
  • Yung Lean and Bladee -- yeah they only get one combined entry actually. It was alright, but not much more than that unfortunately. It was just so much better when I saw them in Globen, or at Way Out West. Bigger, more ambitious performance and better crowd. Here, people didn't even know the lyrics. On the Yung Lean show, there was also a couple geeking on some kind of substance, just kinda ruining the vibe for everyone around them. They were pushy, tried to start fights, flicked me in the back of the head because I had told them they were annoying. When the show started though, a guy got so tired of them he pushed them all the way to the back. Then everybody, even Yung Lean, clapped. In total: 5/10, I don't regret going to any of the concerts, but I would also not be sad if I missed them. Fun to have seen them in Montreal though.

Courses

  • Guided Waves in Photonics -- Learning about waveguides and optical fibers (eg. internet cables and stuff). So far, quite a bad course. Bad professor and not that interesting. He makes everything way too complicated compared to other courses that I find on the same subject online. There are also mini-projects in matlab that we have to do, where he gives us functions that we have to use, but they are hidden. So, we cannot see how they work and if they even work at all. It took probably 15 hours to finish the first project, and it was supposed to take 5. Classes are also really bad, he has powerpoints filled to the brim with text and equations, but he doesn't talk about the contents there at all. Instead he blabs on about his research in the field and scribbles new equations on the slides with his writing pad. Probably among the worst courses I've done. 3/10, just because waveguides are cool and the second project was a lot shorter than the first.
  • Quantum Optics -- very hard to gauge if the course is good or not. It is for sure quantum and optics. I have gotten a lot of practice in quantum mechanics, which is good, but the first professor has been quite flummig and not super coherent. He oscillates between different subjects like the simple harmonic oscillator on a mix of different energy eigenstates. Homework is also incredibly hard, and takes way more time than it should. Atleast it is good training. Second half seems cooler though, and actually a lot less quantum. The professor has also changed, and hopefully he is more coherent. In total: 7/10, because it is done all on the board (so far) and I actually like quantum mechanics.
  • Lasers -- Interesting subject, but the teacher is super incoherent. But so far, among the cooler courses I am doing, just based on what I am learning. I just want the professor to get a good nights sleep before the classes so that he can be a bit more awake and ready to teach. 6/10.
  • Microfabrication -- My expectations for this course was sooo high, but so far it's kind of a disappointment. We are learning about different way and technologies to create things on a micro-and nanometer scale. Which is very cool in itself, but it has been more of just an overview of different techniques. This has been my dream to work in since i was like 14, and I think I have come to the realisation that most parts of this just isn't for me. I definitely think lithography, where you use light to create superduper small things, is really cool but I haven't really gotten a good deep dive in the subject in the way I wanted. Maybe the right way is through laser physics and quantum optics. Also, thin film science (creating super thin layers of metals and stuff), and etching (where you remove the right parts of the super thin layer you just created) honestly seems super boring. It is also all done on powerpoint, which is a big negative. However, along the course we are also doing our own microthingymajig step-by-step in labs. So we can be in the school's cleanroom and do our own process, which is really cool. And the professor is really cool. In total: 6/10, but I might have expected way too much out of this.

me when the feature size of my sample is less than 3 micrometers

  •  Life Cycle Analysis -- this course I took kind of just on a whim, because I had a schedule conflict between two other courses. In large, it's a good course, good professors, lots of practice sessions (compared to zero for the other courses), and alongside we are doing our own life cycle assessment -- meaning, we are investigating and comparing the total climate impact between two things. In our case, a cargo e-bike vs. electric vans for delivery. My project group is also really good and everything. Just kinda sad that this kind of engineering is not my cup of tea. In total: 8/10. It's a really good course, but it's kinda in the wrong field for me, and lectures are still on powerpoint.
  • Bonus entry !!!! Kvantmekanik 10 hp Avancerad nivå 1FA352 -- yeah im taking a course at Uppsala at the same time. I already did kind of all the reading for this course during the summer to be ready for quantum optics, but never did it super in-depth. My plan is to read up on the last part of the course when I'm done with everything here, and when I come back to Sweden, and hopefully I can pass the exam. That would help alot with next autumn, but we'll see how it goes. I also did the midterm in that course yesterday. It was online at 9am swedish time, meaning I had to wake up at 3 and write a quantum mechanics exam. I think I did great, but I was also barely awake for the entire 3 hours that I wrote it. Atleast I think I'll pass it. Based on the canvas page, it seems to be a good course and I'm honestly kinda bummed out I'll miss it if I end up passing the exam in January. Overall 10/10.

Food and Restaurants

  • Schwartz -- Smoked meat deli shop I visisted with Madeline. Quite disappointing and quite expensive. It is apparently owned by Celine Dion, who helped them out of a bankrupcy. We don't see the vision. also 15CAD was quite alot for what we got. 3/10
  • Bagels
    • Fairmount Bagel -- probably the most hipster bagel place in town. They have queues to a bagel place, and is open 24/7. Also very old and the place to find Montreal style bagels. Are they any good? Yeah, kind of. For a bakery, it's not outrageously expensive (about 1.40CAD per bagel) and they have lots of cool variants. However, I have come to realize that I don't really like Montreal style bagels. They're just too sweet. 5/10
    • S:t Viateur Bagel -- The second most hipster bagel place in town, and is fighting tooth and nail against Fairmount. Same kinda ordeal. Also open 24/7, has queues, same price and everything. Again, I'm probably just not the target audience for Montreal style bagels. 5/10
    • No name brand -- Very cheap, which is kind of expected. The brand is the ICA Basic of Canada. However, their bagels are quite nice, and as a non-connosieur they are really good. I have tried both the all dressed and full grain bagels, and both are really good. 8/10
    • The one brand that me and madeline bought that was new york style but italian and all-dressed -- Also very good. More expensive than the No-name brand, but also definitively better. Probably the best bagel I have had so far in Montreal (meaning, also, in my life). 9/10
  • McDonald's -- honestly awful. I ate here morning I had to go back to Sweden, and it was on the verge of vomit inducing. Also the vibe was just not it. I miss max </3 1/10
  • Chez Claudette -- Apparently the best poutine place in town. I honestly don't see the vision here. The fries were soggy and they just kinda forgot salt. Though, the portions are really large. 2/10
  • Beaver Tail -- chain restaurant that sells beaver tails (fried dough with sweet toppings) and poutine. The beaver tails are very similar to churros, they're alright I guess. But the poutine !!! Actually the best I've had in Canada. Although, it's just fries, cheese and gravy, so nothing breathtaking, but it is the first poutine I've had in Canada that I actually enjoyed. 7/10
  • Polytechnique's Canteens -- honestly really good for the price. I'm paying like 10CAD to get a poké bowl, or burger/BLT with fries, or a ramen bowl. It's nothing breathtaking, but for the price it's really good. The actual canteen is in an american high school style, which maybe isn't the most fun environment, but that's a small parenthesis. 8/10
  • Iconoglace -- Hipster ice cream sundae spot close to me. Huge creations and they are really good. But almost too huge, if you're not sharing. Also quite expensive -- 11cad for an ice cream. Though, considering the amount I guess it does make sense. 8/10.
  • Tim Hortons -- honestly, both a letdown and a letup. I thought it was more of a breakfast place, with pancakes and waffles and everything. Turns out they just kinda sell everything and nothing at the same time. It's more of a McDonald'sified café. Me and Madeline has some munchkins, a bagel, muffin and a pizza from here. Everything was alright and actually not vomit-inducing, but nothing crazy. The munchkins were dry. Atleast it was quite cheap. 4/10

Also, I'm think about doing some recipes on the blog aswell. Stay tuned for that!

End of blogpost photodump

and here is the squirrellposting

entry.title: Quick Montreal Trip and Erik + Madeline Reunion! (Madeline’s Blog Takeover)
entry.summary: Hello, cutiepie blog! It’s Madeline, and I have just returned from a wonderful trip to see Erik in Montreal. You know what that means… I get to take over the blog for this week! Yippie! I couldn’t sleep very much on the plane, and, since…
entry.content:
Hello, cutiepie blog!

It’s Madeline, and I have just returned from a wonderful trip to see Erik in Montreal. You know what that means… I get to take over the blog for this week! Yippie!

Day 1

I couldn’t sleep very much on the plane, and, since I had left at midnight from California, I was running on only one hour of sleep once I arrived in Montreal. There was a long walk from the metro station to Erik’s apartment, but I couldn’t complain, even if I was half asleep. It was so lovely to walk through the endless tree-lined streets, filled with brick buildings and students chatting and rushing to who-knows-where. The autumn colors were so vibrant, and the piercing cold was a nice change from the heat in California.

After a long nap at Erik’s place, I went to his school to meet him. The bus ride was just as lovely as my earlier walk, and I sat outside of his school once I arrived, admiring the sunny autumn day. Eventually, he called me after he had finished his test, and I made a very sleepy attempt to go up the insane amount of stairs in his school. After what seemed like an endless journey, I turned a corner and, suddenly, there he was! We ran to meet each other (scaring some random guy in the process) and stood and hugged each other for so long. It was so perfect, with the cold wind and the warm sun and the leaves and us together again.

We went on a long walk up to Mont Royal, passing by such a beautiful graveyard and a lake with many cute squirrels and seagulls. We had such a fun time adventuring. At the top of the lookout, we stood for such a long time just admiring the view. It was so beautiful that I never wanted to leave. The city looked so peaceful.

Eventually, though, we went back down again, walking the entire way back. We stopped by a cute book shop and then went home. I met all his roommates for the first time, and they were all so kind and welcoming! I was very grateful that they all made me feel so comfortable. We made galettes and brownies together, and I ate and conversated while mostly asleep. Eventually, we went to sleep, and it was the end of a wonderful first day.

Day 2

The next morning, we had a little galette breakfast and then went grocery shopping for some bagels. We came back and had a really yummy bagel lunch together and forced his roommates to try some Sour Patch Kids sugar cookies we got for $1. Afterwards, he went to class, and I went to the Montreal Museum of Fine Arts where I spent hours looking at all the beautiful oil paintings (and avoiding the modern art).

Erik met me downtown when he was finished with his class, and we did a bit of shopping. We went to Krispy Kreme and Erik tried it for the first time. I also forced him to wear the cute little hat. After that, we went to the Gay Village, got some things at the dollar store, wandered around, and then went to Old Montreal. We saw the whole skyline just after the sun had set, and the entire sky was pink behind the buildings. We meandered down to the main old street where we listened to music, got some matching post cards, ate some free apples that we got in a shop, and talked for such a long time. It was such a cute and lively area!

Afterwards, we took the metro to a different area closer to Erik’s place where we got some smoked meat sandwiches at the deli shop that Celine Dion owns. It was pretty good, but our consensus was that it wasn’t worth the money and was nothing too special. But it was fun to try once! On the way home, we got some pistachio and raspberry ice cream and ate outside before going home and going to sleep.'

Day 3

We woke up and had breakfast before going out to grab a donut and heading to Little Italy. We spent a few hours at the market, looking at all the shops and debating on whether I should buy maple syrup or not. Erik bought a cute little monstera plant, which is yet to be named (sorry, Sandro, but it must be named).

The buses were at strange spots, so we decided to just walk back instead of trying to search for them. But, luckily for us, the weather was great, and we came across so many interesting shops. There was one we found with two cute dogs just running around which I spent so much time petting, and some others that had cats and were definitely owned by throuple lesbians (iconic).

We had a bagel lunch again once we got back and then went back out again to do some more shopping. We went to so many thrift shops and had a lot of fun going through the beautiful autumn avenues with all the hipster cafes and people wandering about. I bought a butterfly chain belt, and then we walked back again towards Erik’s apartment to grab some groceries. I even found some Peace Tea, a total throwback, so we obviously purchased a couple of cans along with an Arizona Iced Tea.

I did my makeup when we got back and Erik cooked pasta for us, and then we rushed off to the Bladee concert. The crowd was so overwhelming at the venue and there were so many tall and insanely annoying guys pushing me out of the way and going directly in front of us. Luckily for me, I can speak Swedish, so I had a great time talking shit about them right behind their sweaty backs. Later in the concert, Erik and I decided to go on a mission to get to the front, so I happily pushed through and elbowed all of the guys who had pushed me out of the way earlier. They were also so rude and so boring! Everyone was just recording and standing there. No moshing, no nothing. We got pretty close to the front which I was very proud of. Apparently, on the way out, Erik said he heard some guys talking bad about me since I had pushed them out of the way, even though they were the same ones who pushed me earlier. Little did they know, I was talking shit about them way earlier. It’s also kind of a skill issue if you’re getting beaten out and pushed around by a 175cm girl. It’s like a reward that they were so mad at me xoxo. But Bladee was great anyways, despite having a freaky fanbase sometimes. (Unfortunately, however, he did not play Mallwhore Freeestyle or I’m Goofy. A sad, sad day for humanity.)

Day 4

We got up early the next morning and took the bus downtown, where we got some quick Tim Hortons. I was super surprised by the fact that their breakfast sandwich didn’t make me want to vomit (I’m looking at you, McMuffins), and the other little snacks we got were so good! We went to the central bus station and took a long bus ride to Quebec City.

When we got there, we spent forever trying to figure out a walking tour, but eventually went off to see the sights. We saw so many things, like the parliament building, the Plains of Abraham, and so much pretty architecture. We stopped to eat for a bit on the main street where Erik had Wetzel’s Pretzels for the first time, and then we were off again!

We went back on the Plains to look out at the river, and then around where we found a perfect view of the Chateau Frontenac. It was so stunning in the sunlight. We walked down to a little church and to a bustling vendor street, stopping in a little tourist shop to buy some things before exploring the inside of the Chateau Frontenac. It was absolutely gorgeous. We stood by the outlook over the river as the sun set, and then went to go see some street performers who were dressed as lamp people. It was so beautiful and it felt like we were in some vintage children’s storybook, just wandering the streets and seeing mystical performances under pink and orange skies.

We walked through the streets, stopping in little shops along the way. We eventually came across some circus performers who were doing such amazing tricks that we were becoming genuinely concerned about their safety. It was so fun, though.

Afterwards, we searched for a place to eat, eventually settling on poutine from Beaver Tails. It honestly tasted amazing, probably partly because I was so exhausted. We went up to the hostel room after we had eaten and spent hours brainrotting together on Instagram reels and watching random Youtube videos. It was such a fun time, and we had such a wonderful and eventful day together.

Day 5

Erik had to work hard to wake me up the next morning because I was so tired. But, eventually, after many wonderful renditions of “Hotel Breakfast” by Bladee, I woke up, and we went downstairs to have some breakfast. It was very good, and we grabbed some extra things to have for lunch as well.

After we eventually packed up and checked out, we went to the lower part of the city to see a lot of cute shopping streets and then made our way over to the Civilization Museum. There was one half of the museum that was entirely based on indigenous populations in Canada which was so interesting and emotional to see. We took so long getting through that part of the museum and we both learned so much! We had a quick lunch break, and then quickly went through the second half of the museum.

After, we went back up the streets again to the main center to wander around and see the Chateau Frontenac and its lookout again. Once we felt we had looked long enough, we made our way back towards the main bus stop and stopped by a very cute and cozy poke bowl shop. We both got Korean poke bowls and ate entirely in silence. It was so delicious. We went by a store to grab some snacks for the road and then went to the bus stop where we ate and talked for an hour. Then we took the long bus ride back to Montreal and walked back to Erik’s apartment after it had just finished raining.

Day 6

After a cozy breakfast and a slow morning, Erik and I took the bus up to Mont Royal to see the lookout again. It was so beautiful, just as it was before. Then, we went back down the other side of the mountain through the gorgeous orange forest. We stopped by McGill to look at the campus, and it was so pretty! Just as we were about to leave, we saw this girl who was carrying a Normal bag with a Smash chocolate bar inside. We ran up to her to ask about it. She said she was from Norway, gave us the chocolate bar instantly (despite us saying a thousand times that it’s okay), and stood there talking with us for an hour. It was a lot of fun!

After we left, Erik and I went to the Barbie Expo, which I was so excited to see! All of the dolls were so gorgeous, and I wanted to take all of them home. Then, we wandered about trying to find some food, eventually settling on a pizza and blackberry yuzu lemonade from Tim Hortons.

We took the bus to the airport and ate and talked. When we got there, we got a little chicken bowl to eat together. Then we walked over to the U.S. departures area.

erik intermission: they played the subway by chappell roan on the airport speaker systme💀 who is the sad lesbian who though that was a fitting song

We sat and talked for a while, and it felt so sad to say goodbye all over again. But it also felt okay knowing that we had done long distance before, and that it would be alright in the end! I’m so grateful that I took this trip to visit him. It was so lovely and perfect, and the most successful trip I think I’ve ever taken! It was so wonderful to be with Erik again, even if just for a short while. And, after all, it’s only nine and a half weeks until we see each other again. So, we said goodbye to each other, but it’s okay! I’m so happy we got to have a few days together. I had the best time.

Thank you, guys, for reading about our little adventure! I hope it was fun to read through. Love you all xoxo.
